The engineers were doing their jobs correctly; the system failed because the system of which they were a module had been compromised upstream.
themes.implications: The Widder & Nafus finding should change every AI governance training program: it's not enough to train developers to care more about AI safety. You need to change the incentive structures, information flows, and organizational boundaries that currently make AI supply chain responsibility feel like someone else's job.

themes.name: The AI Audit Gap: Why Current AI Audit Regimes Would Not Have Caught LiteLLM
themes.description: Birhane et al. (p140) taxonomize AI audit practices and find they focus primarily on model behavior (fairness, accuracy, safety outputs). The LiteLLM compromise was a software supply chain attack on the build infrastructure — invisible to any audit that evaluates model outputs, training data, or API behavior. This reveals a fundamental gap in the AI audit ecosystem: it has developed around the AI model as the unit of analysis, but the actual attack surface of AI systems is the full software stack from dependencies to deployment infrastructure. Raji et al. (p142) explain why this gap persists: auditors face structural pressures to assess what's measurable and reportable, not what's consequential. Model fairness metrics are concrete and publishable; build pipeline security is abstract and operationally sensitive. The AEF-1 standard (p143) addresses evaluator access conditions, but no current AI audit standard requires examination of the supply chain that produces the AI system. This gap means that financial institutions relying on AI audits for assurance may have false confidence: they have audited the model, not the system. The structural fix requires integrating software supply chain security standards (like SLSA, SPDX AI BOM) into AI audit scope.
themes.implications: Financial institutions should not accept AI audit reports as sufficient assurance for AI supply chain security. AI audit scope must include software supply chain practices: dependency management, build pipeline security, CI/CD configuration, and sub-processor visibility. This is a gap that existing AI governance frameworks do not address.

themes.name: From Point-in-Time Certification to Continuous Trust: Why AI Trust Properties Degrade and What That Means for Governance
themes.description: Grogan's AgentFacts (p146) introduces the critical concept of dynamic metadata with time-to-live freshness — the recognition that AI agent capabilities, permissions, and compliance status change over time. This challenges the prevailing model of AI trust: point-in-time certification (annual audit, upfront due diligence, one-time vendor assessment). The LiteLLM incident illustrates the structural problem with point-in-time approaches: a library that was clean at procurement could be compromised between one update cycle and the next. The AEF-1 standard (p143) implicitly acknowledges this by requiring ongoing access conditions, not just initial assessment. Homewood et al. (p144) introduce the question of when compliance reviews occur: pre-deployment (point-in-time), periodic (low-frequency), or continuous (high-frequency). Continuous approaches are most appropriate for dynamic AI systems but are also most resource-intensive. The governance question this raises: what is the right certification-refresh frequency for AI systems that can change behavior with every model update, fine-tuning, or dependency change? The financial services answer likely varies by risk tier: high-stakes AI systems require continuous or near-continuous assurance; lower-stakes systems can accept periodic review. But the sector currently lacks the infrastructure to deliver continuous assurance for most AI systems.
themes.implications: The point-in-time certification model is inadequate for AI systems that degrade or change continuously. Financial institutions need to develop risk-tiered refresh requirements for AI trust properties: some things should be certified once (fundamental model architecture), some should be monitored continuously (API behavior in production), and some should be assessed on change (fine-tune or update events).

themes.name: MAS TPRM vs APRA CPS 230/234: Neither Framework Was Built for AI Software Supply Chain Risk
themes.description: The MAS Consultation Paper on TPRM (March 2026) extends existing outsourcing guidelines to cover all third-party arrangements, including AI. However, the framework is grounded in traditional service outsourcing logic: risk assessment, due diligence, contracting, monitoring, and termination. The AI BOM approach (p145) and AgentFacts KYA standard (p146) reveal that these frameworks have a fundamental blind spot: they focus on the vendor relationship (is the vendor reliable?) rather than the product provenance (what's actually inside the AI system the vendor is providing?). The LiteLLM case would not be addressed by MAS TPRM requirements: a financial institution could have conducted perfect due diligence on LiteLLM the company, signed ironclad contracts with LiteLLM, and still been compromised by a malicious PyPI package that LiteLLM unknowingly used. APRA CPS 230 and CPS 234 similarly focus on operational risk and security controls at the organizational level, not on the software supply chain that produces AI systems. Neither framework requires AI BOMs, software provenance documentation, or continuous monitoring of AI system components. The gap: financial regulators are applying industrial-age third-party risk frameworks to digital-age AI supply chains, and the mismatch creates exploitable vulnerability.
themes.implications: Regulators and consultants need to develop AI-specific third-party risk management frameworks that incorporate software provenance, AI BOM requirements, and continuous sub-processor monitoring. Existing TPRM frameworks provide necessary but not sufficient coverage.

themes.name: Verification Collapse: Synthetic Reality Attacks the Conditions of Trust, Not Just the Content of Claims
themes.description: Trust in AI degrades not only when outputs are wrong, but when the surrounding verification environment is polluted by synthetic content, synthetic identity, and synthetic interaction. In that environment, provenance becomes harder to establish, institutional verification routines become costlier, and trust claims lose checkability even when individual artifacts look plausible.
themes.implications: AI trust programs should treat provenance, identity, and verification workflow as core control infrastructure. A model cannot be called trustworthy if the institution cannot still verify what inputs, actors, and interactions are real.

themes.name: Oversight Must Follow Verifyability, Not Workflow Position
themes.description: The strongest cross-source pattern in this session is that human oversight only works where humans can still add independent evaluative agency. Better traces, more interface detail, or nominal review steps do not guarantee better judgment. Meaningful oversight depends on solve–verify asymmetry, role-specific harm models, and concentration of human attention at points where review is tractable and decision-changing.
themes.implications: Governance should stop specifying oversight in spatial terms (“in the loop”) and instead specify where humans can still verify, challenge, reroute, or stop outcomes with realistic authority and evidence.

themes.name: From Traceability to Compensation Readiness: Borderless AI Harms Outgrow Local Fault Models
themes.description: As AI harms become transboundary and supply-chain embedded, liability regimes built around local fault attribution and individual human review become less adequate. The governance burden shifts toward foreseeable design responsibility, pooled or channelled compensation mechanisms, and institutional readiness to remediate harms that cannot be cleanly traced to one reviewer or one jurisdiction.
themes.implications: Liability for agentic AI should attach more to design, deployment, monitoring, and compensation architecture than to the fiction that each harmful act could have been individually reviewed by a human.

themes.name: Trust Property Decomposition: Temporal Degradation Profiles Are First-Principles, Not Framework Detail
themes.description: The supervisor's provenance question has a first-principles answer: trust properties decompose into categories with fundamentally different temporal dynamics. (1) Design-Time-Certifiable properties: interpretability architecture, fairness constraints built into model structure, privacy mechanisms (differential privacy), formal verification of safety properties. These are properties of the model's structure, not its behavior, and can be assessed before deployment. (2) Continuously-Monitored properties: calibration drift, alignment stability, robustness under distribution shift, emergent behaviors in new contexts. These are behavioral properties that degrade over time and require post-deployment process monitoring. (3) Change-Triggered properties: performance on new task types, robustness to adversarial adaptation, competence on new data distributions. These can only be assessed at the moment of change (new deployment context, model update, fine-tuning event). The key implication from Ferrario (p093) and Lukas (p095): in Shifting Regime domains (finance, medicine, policy), even the 'certifiable once' properties require post-deployment monitoring because the deployment environment itself changes the effective properties of the system. The Fidelity Paradox shows that training accuracy is not just an unreliable proxy for robustness — it is inversely correlated with robustness in non-stationary domains.
themes.implications: Stop using 'trust framework maturity' as the primary lens. Instead: decompose each trust claim into its temporal properties and apply the appropriate governance mechanism — certification, continuous monitoring, or change-triggered assessment.

themes.name: Reliance Is a Risk-Management Proxy, Not Trust: Why This Distinction Changes Everything
themes.description: The philosophical literature (McLeod, Durán & Pozzi p101) makes a sharp distinction: trust is an attitude; trustworthiness is a property; reliance is the behavioral outcome. In AI contexts, reliance is what happens when epistemic access is too costly or unavailable — you use proxies (model cards, SOC 2 audits, benchmark scores, regulatory certifications) to manage the risk of relying on the AI. These proxies are not the same as epistemic access to the AI's trustworthiness. Quattrociocchi (p094) shows why this gap is structural: LLMs produce epistemically ungrounded but linguistically fluent outputs (Epistemia), so surface-level evaluation (benchmark scores, human preference ratings) measures fluency, not reliability. Lukas (p095) shows that even benchmark-proven models can be anti-robust in Shifting Regime domains. The practical implication: organisations that conflate reliance on AI with actual trustworthiness are managing the appearance of trust, not trust itself. The SOC 2 audit is evidence of process reliability, not outcome reliability. The model card is evidence of disclosed properties, not verified alignment. Neither closes the epistemic gap.
themes.implications: Trust programs should be explicit about what they are: risk-management programs for reliance on AI, not epistemic programs for establishing genuine trustworthiness. This changes what you audit, what you monitor, and what you disclose.

themes.name: Meaningful Human Oversight = Operational Skill, Not Documentation: The Aviation Inflection Point
themes.description: The core answer to 'what does meaningful HITL mean at scale' has a first-principles component and a practical component. First-principles: human oversight only works where humans can add independent evaluative agency — the ability to understand, judge, verify, redirect, or substitute the AI's actions (from p148's operative/evaluative agency distinction, confirmed by the Strata p102 HITL analysis). If the human cannot verify the AI's reasoning, they cannot meaningfully oversee it. Second-principles: the aviation parallel. After the 1970s-80s accident wave, airlines did not just add more pilots — they redesigned oversight as an operational skill through CRM: trained judgment, simulator practice, structured procedures, and no-blame debriefs. Enterprise AI is at the same inflection point. organisations that implement HITL as documentation (checklists, approval workflows, oversight diagrams) without training humans to exercise real judgment under realistic pressure have governance theater, not governance. The automation complacency finding (the more reliable the AI appears, the less vigilant the human overseer) means that passive monitoring degrades precisely when it's most needed.
themes.implications: HITL programs need: (1) risk-tiered oversight modes (HITL/HOTL/HOT by decision type), (2) simulation-based training for approvers, (3) time-boxed decision lanes, (4) active mechanisms to combat automation complacency. Documentation-only programs will fail under scrutiny.

themes.name: Agentic AI Liability: Diffused Accountability Creates Structural Gaps, and Courts Are Starting to Close Them
themes.description: The Global Legal Insights analysis (p099) identifies the core problem: in agentic AI supply chains, every actor — developers, data curators, compliance teams, executives — has plausible deniability, producing 'responsibility gaps' where victims cannot identify a defendant. The Thomson Geer analysis (p098) shows that Australian law applies existing doctrines directly, but the non-deterministic behavior of agentic AI complicates the foreseeability and control elements of negligence. The Harvard JOLT analysis (p096) shows courts are moving toward a 'reasonable AI system' standard — evaluating whether the organisation had adequate governance, oversight architecture, and monitoring — rather than asking whether any individual AI action was adequately reviewed. This is a structural shift: liability is migrating from individual action review to systemic governance adequacy. Three concrete solutions are emerging: (1) channeling liability to a single entity (deployer/operator) with mandatory insurance (nuclear liability model), (2) mandatory pre-market certification for high-risk AI, (3) layered responsibility frameworks that proportionally allocate liability across the supply chain. The Air Canada chatbot precedent (p098) shows organisations cannot disclaim responsibility for AI representations.
themes.implications: Financial institutions deploying agentic AI need: (1) explicit authority mapping for AI agents, (2) identity governance that technically enforces approval checkpoints, (3) stress-tested liability scenarios, (4) monitoring systems that create court-defensible evidence of governance adequacy.

themes.name: Singapore MAS Thematic Review Model: Regulatory Empiricism Beats Framework Theorizing
themes.description: Singapore MAS (p100) represents the most concrete success case of regulator-led AI governance improvement. Rather than waiting for a perfect framework, MAS used its existing supervisory powers to conduct a thematic review of banks' actual AI model risk management practices, documented what good practice looked like across institutions, and published it as industry guidance. This is regulatory empiricism: building governance guidance from observed field practice rather than top-down theoretical framework design. The alternative — prescriptive frameworks designed in advance of field observation — consistently produces governance requirements that are either too generic to be useful or too specific to match actual deployment contexts. The MAS model has three distinctive features: (1) it starts from observed practice, not theoretical design; (2) it publishes findings so the industry can self-assess; (3) it updates as practice evolves. For APRA/ASIC: a thematic review of actual AI model risk management practices across major institutions, with published findings and guidance, would be more valuable than new guidance documents that attempt to specify requirements in advance of field evidence.
themes.implications: The most valuable thing a regulator can do for AI governance is observe what good practice actually looks like in the field and publish it. APRA and ASIC should conduct thematic reviews now, even if the frameworks are incomplete.

themes.name: Ground Truth is Always Constructed: The Epistemic Foundation of AI Trust is Socially Negotiated
themes.description: Jaton (2024) demonstrates through ethnographic research embedded with AI teams that ground truth datasets are not objective facts but artefacts of human annotation decisions, negotiations, and cultural assumptions. There is no "raw data" in AI. Every training dataset encodes choices about what counts as correct, how disagreement is resolved, and whose values take precedence. This means AI trust cannot rest on the fiction of objectively verified ground truth. Trust in AI must rest on governance of the construction process — who labeled the data, what assumptions they embedded, how was disagreement handled — not on the pretence of objective ground truth. This is a first-principles challenge to the entire enterprise of AI trust: if the epistemic foundation is constructed, the trust built on it is too.
evidence: Jaton 2024 - Issues in Science and Technology
PhilArchive 2025 - Trust is not a conceptual confusion
Blanco 2025 - trust beyond reliance
themes.implications: First-principles challenge to AI trust: the ground upon which AI trust is built is itself socially constructed. Any AI trust framework must account for this by focusing on the governance of the data construction process, not the pretence of objective ground truth. This has immediate implications for training data documentation, model cards, and data lineage requirements.

themes.name: Reliability is the Correct Aspiration, Not Trustworthiness — Trust Creates Obligations AI Cannot Bear
themes.description: Carroll (2025) builds on Baier's Goodwill Theory to argue that the AI industry's pursuit of "trustworthy AI" is conceptually confused and practically dangerous. Trust involves making oneself vulnerable to another party's goodwill — their motivation to act in the trustor's interests. AI lacks goodwill: it has no motives, no capacity for genuine care, no standing to make moral commitments. "Trustworthy" AI creates human vulnerability without the reciprocal moral capacity that makes trust relationships ethically bearable. The correct aspiration is reliability — competent performance without the normative baggage of trust. This is not just a philosophical point: organisations marketing AI as "trustworthy" are creating trust obligations they cannot discharge, exposing themselves to liability when AI fails in ways that would constitute bad faith if done by a human trustee.
evidence: Carroll 2025 - Philosophy & Technology
Baier 1986 Goodwill Theory
PhilArchive 2025
themes.implications: Critical reframe for AI trust messaging and liability: organisations should position AI as a reliable tool, not a trustworthy agent. The liability implications of the "trustworthy AI" framing are significant — you cannot disclaim what you've marketed. This should change how AI governance frameworks are written and how AI is marketed.

themes.name: Trust Properties are Temporally Degraded — Most Cannot Be Certified Once
themes.description: Multiple sources confirm that trust properties in AI are not stable over time. Model drift (Radkowski 2026), performance degradation, data distribution shifts, and concept drift mean a model trustworthy at certification time is not necessarily trustworthy weeks or months later. "Performance degradation is particularly dangerous because systems often continue operating well enough until significant harm has already occurred." (Radkowski 2026). This has direct implications for the certification question: properties like accuracy, calibration, bias absence, and fairness are fundamentally continuous, not point-in-time. Only structural properties — whether the model has been trained on representative data, whether the architecture includes human override capability — can be certified at a point in time. Dynamic properties require continuous monitoring infrastructure. Any regulatory framework that allows point-in-time certification of dynamic properties is building compliance theatre.
evidence: Radkowski 2026 - Risk Management Magazine
Towards AI 2026 - model releases to reliability
DecodetheFuture - AI for Risk Management
themes.implications: The certification vs continuous monitoring distinction is not academic — it determines what regulators can meaningfully require. Properties that degrade over time (accuracy, fairness, calibration) cannot be certified once. Regulatory frameworks must mandate continuous monitoring for these properties. Audit practices must evolve to distinguish point-in-time assessable properties from continuous ones.

themes.name: The Automation Paradox Makes Naive Human-in-the-Loop Infeasible at Scale
themes.description: Bainbridge's automation paradox (1983) describes how automating tasks degrades human operators' ability to monitor and intervene when automated systems fail. Recent AI-specific research confirms this effect intensifies: the vigilance decrement is one of the most robust findings in attention research; AI review fatigue causes human reviewers to become cognitively overloaded as AI output volumes increase. At agentic AI scale, the volume of AI decisions makes per-action human review mathematically impossible. The naive interpretation of "meaningful human in the loop" — human review of every AI action — is infeasible. The correct interpretation, as articulated by Xu et al. (2025), requires: (1) technical architectures with embedded human control mechanisms; (2) human-AI interactions optimised for human understanding; (3) the right to intervene and trace responsibility. Human oversight must be architectural, not per-action.
evidence: Bainbridge 1983 - Ironies of Automation
Klein and Feltmate 2025 - vigilance decrement
Xu et al. 2025 - Human-controllable AI
themes.implications: Human-in-the-loop requirements in AI governance frameworks are being written on the assumption that humans can meaningfully review AI actions at scale. They cannot. Frameworks must specify the architectural conditions under which human oversight is meaningful — not the volume of human review. Regulators must understand that oversight quality is not proportional to the number of human reviewers.

themes.name: Singapore MindForge as Proof-of-Concept: Industry-Regulator Co-Development Can Produce Implementable AI Governance
themes.description: The supervisor asked whether any organisations are "getting AI trust/governance right." Singapore's MAS MindForge is the clearest affirmative case: 24 financial institutions co-developing an AI Risk Management Toolkit with MAS over multiple phases, producing an Operationalisation Handbook and case studies. Success factors: (1) Industry-regulator co-development distributes governance innovation costs and produces implementable (not just principled) guidance; (2) MAS provided coordination and regulatory legitimacy but did not dictate solutions; (3) The consortium model created shared ownership and peer learning; (4) Practical outputs — operationalisation handbook, case studies — versus abstract principles. The lesson for other jurisdictions: AI governance frameworks produced by regulators alone tend to be either too abstract or too prescriptive. Industry-produced frameworks tend to be too self-serving. The Singapore model suggests a middle path that may be replicable in other contexts.
evidence: MAS March 2026 - MindForge Phase 2 announcement
MAS Agentic AI Framework January 2026
Compliance Week March 2026
themes.implications: Actionable case study for jurisdictions looking to improve AI governance. The consortium model is not universal — it requires willing regulators and competitive-but-cooperative industry players. But it is a replicable design pattern. Advisors working with regulators should advocate for industry-regulator co-development over either pure regulatory dictate or industry self-governance.

themes.name: Verify-Don’t-Trust Provenance: AI Trust is Moving from Narrative Assurance to Evidence Chains
themes.description: The session’s strongest provenance finding is that trust is increasingly being reconstructed as an evidence-chain problem. The IETF lifecycle attestation draft (p155), the VAP framework (p156), and Howard’s chain-of-epistemic-custody model (p157) all reject the old pattern in which providers assert that their systems are safe, well logged, and responsibly governed. Instead, they propose architectures where lineage, completeness, override events, and output provenance can be independently verified. This is not the same as proving that an AI system is substantively good, fair, or truthful. It is narrower but foundational: it makes it possible to prove what system acted, on what evidence, through what path, with what intervention history. The deeper first-principles point is that AI trust is shifting from institutional promise to inspectable evidence. But these frameworks also reveal a limit: provenance begins only at the first trusted boundary. If the source observation is fabricated or the target variable is normatively corrupted, cryptographic integrity only preserves a falsehood faithfully. Therefore the real governance obligation is two-part: build evidence chains after the trusted boundary, and explicitly declare where that boundary starts and what assumptions remain outside it.
themes.implications: Assurance teams should stop making undifferentiated claims about “end-to-end trust.” Instead they should identify the first trusted boundary, distinguish chain integrity from source truth, and require evidence-pack reconstruction capability for high-risk AI decisions.

themes.name: Meaningful Oversight at Scale is a Runtime Control Architecture, Not a Headcount Model
themes.description: The combined lesson from the Harvard JOLT negligence analysis (p158) and the Strata operational HITL article (p159) is that “human in the loop” only means something when humans hold real, technically enforced leverage at the right moments. Oversight fails when law or policy assumes that a human can monitor every action, verify opaque reasoning, and remain vigilant indefinitely. Under agentic conditions, those assumptions collapse. Meaningful oversight therefore cannot be defined by the mere existence of reviewers. It must be defined by runtime control architecture: risk-tiered escalation paths, identity-bound intervention rights, time-bounded approvals, clear shutdown authority, and evidence that humans were given enough context to exercise independent judgment. This theme extends the prior automation-paradox insight by making the design implication explicit. At scale, the relevant question is not “how many humans are involved?” but “where do humans retain causally significant power, and is that power technically and operationally real?”
themes.implications: Regulators and firms should audit intervention capacity, context quality, and enforcement mechanisms rather than counting review steps. Oversight programs should be stress-tested like operational resilience controls, not documented like static approval workflows.

themes.name: Successful AI Governance in Banking Looks Like Operating Model Redesign, Not a Compliance Overlay
themes.description: DBS’s responsible AI materials (p161) and the reporting on its operating-model transformation (p162), read alongside Singapore’s MindForge work (p109), point to a concrete pattern in successful governance. The organisations that appear to be getting AI governance relatively right are not simply adding a responsible-AI committee to existing workflows. They are redesigning platform, process, workforce capability, escalation routes, and economic measurement together. DBS’s PURE framing matters, but only because it is tied to data foundations, model governance, staged adoption, cross-functional review, employee training, and central infrastructure that shortens delivery cycles while preserving oversight. That combination turns governance from a brake into an operating discipline. The first-principles insight is that trust at scale is emergent from organisational design. It is not a property produced by ethics statements alone, and not a property produced by technical testing alone. It emerges when norms, controls, incentives, tooling, and skills are aligned tightly enough that responsible use becomes the default path rather than an afterthought.
themes.implications: For financial institutions, the strategic benchmark should be integrated operating models with embedded governance, not stand-alone responsible-AI documentation. Advisory work should focus on redesigning decision pathways, training, and platform controls together.

themes.name: Trust Properties Have Different Decay Profiles — Structural vs Behavioral
themes.description: The first-principles analysis of trust provenance reveals that AI trust properties decay at fundamentally different rates, requiring different governance treatments. Structural properties — approved architecture, override hooks, lineage controls, role segregation, and other design-level characteristics — are largely stable after deployment and can be meaningfully assessed at a point in time. Behavioral properties — calibration, accuracy, fairness, drift, and capability alignment — change continuously as the world changes, as data distributions shift, and as the model adapts or is updated. The temporal degradation study (Vela et al., p169) provides the empirical grounding: AI model error increases measurably over time since last training, following distinct patterns (gradual, explosive, or increasingly unpredictable). Critically, even feature importance — the relative contribution of input variables to model outputs — changes temporally. This means that a fairness assessment conducted last quarter may be measuring different feature interactions than the model is actually using today. The governance implication is that regulatory frameworks and assurance practices that treat all trust properties the same — requiring point-in-time certification for everything — produce compliance theatre rather than genuine risk management. The correct approach, now beginning to emerge in ETSI's CABCA standard (p168), is to match governance cadence to property decay profile. Structural properties warrant certification cycles measured in years. Behavioral properties warrant continuous monitoring with automated alerting. This is not just an operational distinction — it is a philosophical one. Whether a property can be certified once depends on whether its relationship to ground truth is stable or decaying.
themes.implications: Assurance practices must explicitly categorise each trust property by its decay profile. Regulatory frameworks should mandate continuous monitoring for behavioral properties and point-in-time certification only for structural ones. This distinction also determines what 'ground truth' means operationally for each property type.

themes.name: The Oversight Paradox Amplification: More Reliable AI Makes Human Oversight Harder
themes.description: The Harvard JOLT paper (p165) identifies the core automation paradox: humans are worst at intervening at the moment AI fails precisely because AI rarely fails. Lulled by reliability, operators disengage cognitively — out-of-the-loop unfamiliarity — and cannot recover when the rare failure occurs. But there is a deeper amplification: as AI systems become more reliable AND more capable, the oversight challenge worsens on two axes simultaneously. First, more reliable automation means longer periods of cognitive disengagement between failures, making the human less prepared when intervention is needed. Second, more capable AI means the failure modes are more complex, opaque, and consequential when they occur — the human's cognitive load at the moment of required intervention is higher precisely when their preparation is lower. Xu et al. (p170) formalise this as the Oversight Paradox Amplification: the AI's capability trajectory outruns the human's oversight capacity as both grow. The paradox is that improving AI reliability and capability creates conditions where meaningful human oversight becomes structurally harder, not easier. This is not a paradox to be solved — it is a structural limit to be governed. The only viable response is architectural: building AI systems whose capability growth is deliberately bounded by oversight architecture, so that human control scales with AI capability. The alternative — deploying increasingly capable AI while maintaining 'meaningful human oversight' as a compliance statement — produces the liability sponge problem where humans absorb accountability for failures they had no realistic capacity to prevent.
themes.implications: Firms should not assume that more AI reliability means less oversight risk. The oversight paradox means that as AI improves, the governance architecture must actively strengthen to prevent the human from becoming a progressively less capable control point. This has direct implications for how to allocate investment between AI capability and oversight infrastructure.

themes.name: Liability Sponges and the Accountability Gap: When Mandated Oversight Is Constructively Infeasible
themes.description: The Harvard JOLT analysis (p165) introduces the 'liability sponge' concept with precision: humans positioned in AI oversight roles absorb legal, moral, and reputational impact of system failures without possessing the cognitive architecture to prevent those failures. This is not a metaphor — it is a structural description of how accountability law interacts with automated systems. The Uber Arizona case (2018) is the canonical legal example: the safety driver was charged with negligent homicide while streaming The Voice, but the real failure was automation design that made meaningful oversight impossible. The Sriwijaya Air Flight 182 crash (2021) illustrates the same pattern at system scale: pilots failed to monitor a clearly malfunctioning system for minutes before a fatal dive because automation had so thoroughly deskilled them. The accountability gap emerges when both the human operator and the AI developer can plausibly disclaim responsibility: the operator because they lacked realistic intervention capacity, the developer because the system performed as designed until the specific failure mode occurred. This gap is not a bug that better regulation can close — it is a structural feature of delegating consequential decisions to AI. The legal consequence framework must therefore shift: from attributing fault after the fact to assessing whether the deployment decision itself — including the oversight architecture designed in — was reasonable. This is the deployer's duty articulated in the three-pillar framework (p165): not 'did the human catch this failure?' but 'was the oversight architecture genuinely capable of catching failures of this type?'
themes.implications: Legal and compliance teams should stop asking 'do we have a human in the loop?' and start asking 'is our oversight architecture designed to give humans realistic intervention capacity?' This reframe shifts liability analysis from outcome attribution to deployment decision quality.

themes.name: Trust Is Institutional, Not Algorithmic — the Assurance Target Is the Organisation-AI Pair
themes.description: The philosophical analysis in Lugato and Floridi (p166) provides the conceptual clarification that resolves many practical confusion in AI trust: AI systems can be relied upon but cannot be trusted in the full philosophical sense, because trust requires vulnerability, normative expectations, and the possibility of betrayal by a being with genuine intentions. AI systems lack the relevant moral agency — they have no interests to protect, no goodwill to exercise, and no capacity for betrayal in the morally relevant sense. What can be trusted is the institution deploying and governing the AI. This reframe has profound operational consequences for assurance practice. First, the assurance target is not the AI model but the institution-AI system pair: can this organisation's governance of this AI system justify the reliance we are placing in it? Second, the key measurable is calibration — whether the organisation's reliance on the AI matches its actual trustworthiness. Over-reliance (excessive trust) produces automation bias and missed anomalies. Under-reliance (excessive scepticism) produces deskilling and failure to capture AI's genuine value. Third, trust as a quantitative property of the reliance relationship can be measured: high trust means the organisation invests low monitoring resources relative to the value extracted from the AI. Low trust means monitoring costs approach or exceed value, and the reliance relationship is unsustainable. This reframing does not make the philosophical debate about AI trust irrelevant — it makes it operationally useful by redirecting it toward the question that actually matters for governance.
themes.implications: Assurance engagements should be scoped around institutional governance capacity, not model performance metrics alone. The assessment question is: does this organisation's governance of this AI system justify the reliance stakeholders are placing in it? This requires evaluating decision-rights allocation, escalation paths, monitoring investment, and incentive structures — not just technical model properties.

themes.name: Trust at Scale Emerges from Organisational Design — It Is Not an Output of Ethics Statements or Technical Testing
themes.description: The DBS case study (p167), read alongside Singapore's MindForge work and the broader AI governance literature, produces a first-principles conclusion that should reshape how organisations approach AI trust: at scale, trust is emergent from organisational design, not a property that can be produced independently. The organisations getting AI governance right — DBS, Singapore's consortium banks, a small number of leading global institutions — share a common structural feature: they have redesigned their operating models around AI, not added AI governance to their existing operating models. DBS's PURE framework (Purposeful, Unsurprising, Respectful, Explainable) is useful precisely because it is not a standalone ethics statement — it is embedded in data foundations, model governance protocols, staged rollout, cross-functional review, employee training, and central infrastructure. Each element reinforces the others. Governance becomes an operating discipline rather than a compliance overlay. The organisational design principle is that trust emerges when norms, controls, incentives, tooling, and staff capabilities are aligned tightly enough that responsible AI use becomes the default path — not a special effort requiring willpower or vigilance. This is structurally similar to how safety emerges in high-reliability organisations: it is not produced by having a safety department, but by designing every process, incentive, and tool so that safe behaviour is the path of least resistance.
themes.implications: Leaders should stop asking 'do we have a good AI governance policy?' and start asking 'is our operating model designed so that responsible AI use is the default behaviour?' The audit question is whether norms, controls, incentives, and capabilities are aligned — not whether a responsible AI document exists.

themes.name: Appropriate Reliance — the Operationally Useful Replacement for AI Trust
themes.description: Lugato and Floridi's pragmatic approach (p166) resolves the trust/reliance debate in a way that is directly operationally useful: treat trust as a quantitative property of the reliance relationship, measured by its efficiency. Appropriate reliance has three components: (1) Appropriateness — the reliance is warranted given the system's actual capabilities, which requires calibration (matching trust level to system trustworthiness); (2) Efficiency — the monitoring investment required to maintain appropriate reliance is low relative to the value extracted, which requires the system to be genuinely reliable and the oversight architecture to be well-designed; (3) Calibration — the organisation's trust level matches the system's actual trustworthiness across different contexts and time periods. The measurement approach proposed by Taddeo (2007, 2010) and applied in the Lugato/Floridi framework — measuring trust as a ratio of monitoring investment to expected gains — provides a concrete operational metric. This replaces vague claims about 'trustworthy AI' with quantifiable parameters: trust level, monitoring cost, expected gain, and calibration error. For assurance practice, this means every AI system in a high-stakes domain should have a trust dashboard showing these parameters, updated continuously. The goal is not to maximise trust — overly trusting AI is dangerous. The goal is to calibrate reliance to the system's actual properties, maintain that calibration as both the system and the environment change, and ensure the monitoring investment remains proportionate to the value extracted.
themes.implications: Assurance frameworks should operationalise the three components of appropriate reliance: are systems being used within their validated capability boundaries (appropriateness), is monitoring investment proportionate (efficiency), and do users' trust levels match actual system performance (calibration)? These three questions provide a more useful assessment structure than any binary 'trustworthy or not' framework.

themes.name: Dynamic Authority Reversal Framework — Transitional Accountability for Agentic AI
themes.description: Westover et al. (2026) provide the missing operationalisation for human oversight of agentic AI: the Dynamic Authority Reversal (DAR) framework. The core move is treating authority as a state variable that transitions across four states — Human-Leader/AI-Follower (HL), AI-Leader/Human-Follower (AL), Co-Leadership (CO), and Mutual Override (MO) — rather than a fixed allocation. Transitions are triggered by four signal classes: data superiority (AI predictive performance exceeds defined margin), contextual judgment requirements (tacit knowledge or relational factors predominate), risk thresholds (potential harms exceed tolerance), and ethics overrides (normative constraints demand human primacy). Hysteresis bands prevent oscillation by requiring exit thresholds to differ from entry thresholds. Safe-exit timers guarantee human review after maximum dwell time in any state, directly addressing the EU AI Act's 'meaningful oversight' requirement without specifying it at the architectural level. The Reversal Register is the critical governance artifact: an auditable log binding each decision to its prevailing authority state, trigger conditions, and justificatory explanation. This transforms accountability from a static property (who signed off) to a dynamic property (which authority state produced each decision, and why). For agentic AI financial services applications (CBA Compass AI, Mastercard Agent Pay), DAR provides the governance architecture that makes 'meaningful human oversight' operational rather than aspirational.
themes.implications: Every high-stakes AI system in financial services should implement an Authority-State Playbook specifying: the four possible authority states, entry/exit trigger conditions with hysteresis bands, maximum dwell time in AL state (triggering mandatory safe-exit human review), and a Reversal Register logging all state transitions. Regulators should accept Reversal Register logs as evidence of 'meaningful oversight' under EU AI Act Article 14.

themes.name: Resolution-Matched Human Supervision — the Architectural Fix for HITL at Scale
themes.description: The core architectural insight from this research session: human oversight fails at agentic AI scale not because humans are insufficiently capable but because the resolution of human oversight is mismatched to the resolution of AI action. Humans can meaningfully oversee decisions that are discrete, low-frequency, and high-stakes — the traditional HITL model. Agentic AI produces decisions that are continuous, high-frequency, and varied in stakes — exceeding human processing capacity at every point. The architectural fix is resolution-matched supervision: for each class of AI action, design the minimal human oversight intervention that maintains accountability without requiring human review of every token. This means: (1) synchronous HOTL with hysteresis bands for real-time high-frequency monitoring (human reviews summaries and exceptions, not every action); (2) Reversal Registers as the accountability artifact (each decision bound to authority state, not each token bound to human approval); (3) safe-exit timers as the mandatory human review trigger (maximum dwell time in AI-lead state before human review is required by architecture, not by policy). This is not a governance workaround — it is an architectural solution to an architectural problem.
themes.implications: The question practitioners should ask is not 'how much human oversight do we need?' but 'what is the minimum oversight architecture that maintains accountability for each class of AI action?' Over-scoping human oversight wastes resources and produces rubber-stamping. Under-scoping produces accountability voids. Resolution-matched supervision is the design principle that sits between these failure modes.

themes.name: Constitutive vs. Descriptive Knowledge Sources — the Epistemic Architecture of AI Trust
themes.description: Gazit (2026) provides the most important philosophical contribution to AI trust theory since Taddeo (2007): the distinction between constitutive knowledge sources (which create normative reality through institutional authority) and descriptive knowledge sources (which describe pre-existing reality and can be evaluated by correspondence). Strongly constitutive sources — statutes, diagnostic criteria, central bank announcements — establish what counts as valid within their domain through institutional authority, not through correspondence to external truth. When an AI system grounds its outputs in constitutive sources, it inherits the institutional authority of those sources without requiring algorithmic transparency. Descriptive sources — technical standards, practice guidelines — provide reliable guidance but cannot constitute what counts as valid. This distinction resolves the 'opacity paradox': users cannot directly assess AI reasoning, but they can assess whether the AI is operating within constitutive frameworks (law, regulation, diagnostic criteria) that have recognized epistemic authority. The implication: AI trust frameworks should be designed to map AI outputs to constitutive source categories, not to achieve transparency that exceeds human cognitive capacity.
themes.implications: Assurance practitioners should map every high-stakes AI decision domain to its constitutive knowledge sources. For credit decisions: APRA/EBA regulatory standards constitute creditworthiness, not statistical models. For medical AI: clinical diagnostic criteria constitute diagnosis, not training data patterns. For financial AI: regulatory reporting standards constitute compliance, not model outputs. AI systems that map to constitutive sources inherit institutional epistemic warrant.

themes.name: Oversight Fatigue Cascade — the Quantitative Logic of HITL Failure
themes.description: Choubey (2026) and Langer et al. (2019) together provide the quantitative logic of why HITL fails at agentic AI scale. The oversight fatigue cascade has four stages: (1) automation complacency — human trust in AI increases with AI reliability, decreasing the cognitive effort allocated to oversight; (2) alert desensitisation — the ratio of false alarms to genuine signals exceeds human cognitive filtering capacity, producing alert fatigue; (3) reflexive approval — humans apply pattern-matching heuristics ('this looks like prior approved cases') without genuine evaluation; (4) accountability ritual — oversight becomes a compliance artifact signed by a human who did not substantively review. Langer et al.'s quantitative model shows that oversight is mathematically insufficient when the overseer cannot match the system's error rate with higher sensitivity. For agentic AI making thousands of decisions per second, the oversight failure is not a governance failure — it is a mathematical inevitability given the ratio of AI action frequency to human cognitive bandwidth. The solution is not more oversight; it is architectural redesign.
themes.implications: Any AI governance framework that relies on increasing the frequency or depth of human oversight for agentic AI is building on a structurally unsound foundation. The correct response is: (1) acknowledge that oversight will fail; (2) design forensically auditable systems so that failures can be detected and corrected retroactively; (3) implement resolution-matched supervision as the architectural alternative to oversight-by-review.

themes.name: Short TIAS — a Practical Trust Calibration Measurement Instrument
themes.description: McGrath et al. (2025) provide the first psychometrically validated short-form measurement instrument for AI trust calibration: the 3-item Short TIAS (S-TIAS). The items (likely derived from the 12-item TIAS via factor analysis) capture the two dimensions of trust and distrust as oblique (related but distinct) factors. Key findings: (1) S-TIAS is valid and reliable for measuring trust in AI across diverse applications (self-driving cars, medical diagnosis, virtual assistants); (2) machine trust propensity (MTP) is a significant predictor of AI trust under high-performance conditions; (3) trust scores significantly predict behavioural intention to use AI beyond demographic variables and propensities; (4) trust calibration failure (over-trust or under-trust) is measurable and detectable. The practical value: organisations can now measure whether their users' trust in AI systems is calibrated to actual system performance using a 3-item instrument administered at multiple time points. This enables longitudinal trust calibration monitoring — exactly what the CABCA framework requires for behavioural trust properties.
themes.implications: Every financial services AI system should include S-TIAS measurement at deployment, 90 days, and annually. Discrepancies between measured trust and system performance data indicate calibration problems requiring intervention. S-TIAS scores should be included in model performance dashboards.

themes.name: Trajectory-Based Liability — the Accountability Framework for Agentic AI
themes.description: Bommarito et al. (2026) identify the fundamental limitation of existing AI liability frameworks when applied to agentic AI: individual AI actions are not the unit of harm. Agentic AI produces harm through accumulated trajectories of behaviour — sequences of decisions that individually may be unremarkable but collectively produce harmful outcomes. A single database query is not harmful; a sequence of queries that gradually exfiltrates data is. Traditional liability frameworks attach to individual actions or output states and cannot capture trajectory-level harm. Trajectory-based liability restructures accountability around: (1) trajectory logging — comprehensive logs of all actions, decisions, and environmental interactions sufficient to reconstruct full behavioural trajectories; (2) trajectory analysis — regulators and courts assess whether trajectories stayed within permissible bounds; (3) trajectory-based standards of care — the applicable standard is not whether any individual action was acceptable but whether the overall trajectory was. March 2026 updates to the AI Liability Directive shift the burden of proof for autonomous AI harm to deployers, meaning deployers must demonstrate their agentic systems did not produce harmful trajectories. For financial services, trajectory logging infrastructure is becoming a legal necessity.
themes.implications: Organisations deploying agentic AI should implement trajectory logging infrastructure now, not as a governance best practice but as legal risk management. The burden of proof shift means that in any AI-related harm dispute, the absence of trajectory logs will be treated as evidence of inadequate oversight.

themes.name: CABCA Standard — Continuous Conformity Assessment as the Operational Replacement for Point-in-Time Certification
themes.description: ETSI TS 104 008 (CABCA) is the operational standard that resolves the structural tension between dynamic AI systems and static regulatory frameworks. CABCA treats change as an expected condition rather than an exception, building assessment processes around continuous evidence collection and automated reporting. The key architectural features: (1) change detection triggers reassessment automatically, without waiting for scheduled audits; (2) machine-readable specifications operationalise high-level regulatory requirements into measurable metrics; (3) the full lifecycle of conformity assessment is covered — from initial deployment through post-market monitoring. The EU AI Act's August 2026 enforcement date makes CABCA immediately relevant: high-risk AI system operators need a methodology for demonstrating continuous compliance, not just point-in-time self-assessment. CABCA provides the blueprint. The standard is not yet a harmonised standard under EU law (making compliance voluntary), but it represents the operational state-of-the-art for continuous AI conformity assessment and is likely to become the de facto standard for sophisticated AI operators.
themes.implications: Forward-looking financial services firms should adopt CABCA methodology now, ahead of harmonised standard publication. The investment in continuous monitoring infrastructure that satisfies CABCA requirements will position firms for EU AI Act compliance and provide superior assurance evidence compared to point-in-time assessment.

themes.name: Ground Truth vs. Assumptions vs. Proxies in AI Trust Certification
themes.description: Synthesising across Gazit (constitutive sources), Taddeo (trust as efficiency ratio), and McGrath (trust measurement): the certification chain for AI trust contains three types of epistemic content. Ground truth: the actual performance of the AI system, which is unknowable in principle for opaque systems and measurable only through behavioural testing. Assumptions: the beliefs about AI performance that organisations hold, typically based on validation results at deployment, which degrade as the system encounters novel data or as the environment changes. Proxies: the measurable indicators used to stand in for ground truth — accuracy on test sets, calibration curves, fairness metrics, drift detection statistics. The critical insight is that certification documents typically certify proxies (accuracy on a validation dataset) while users and operators assume they certify ground truth. This gap — certified proxies vs. assumed ground truth — is the structural source of most AI trust failures. The appropriate response is to make the proxy-to-ground-truth assumption explicit in every certification document: what proxy was measured, under what conditions, and what assumptions connect this to actual performance?
themes.implications: Every AI certification or assurance statement should explicitly state: (1) what was measured (the proxy); (2) under what conditions it was measured; (3) what assumptions connect the proxy to actual performance; (4) what the known degradation rate is; (5) what the monitoring frequency is. Certificates should never state accuracy without the proxy qualification.

themes.name: CBA AI Governance Model — Australia's First Enterprise-Wide AI Governance Framework
themes.description: Commonwealth Bank of Australia's December 2025 public AI approach report represents the most complete enterprise-wide AI governance disclosure from any Australian financial institution. Key features: (1) dedicated governance forum for AI risk framework development; (2) Group AI Policy with six principles (environmental and social, fairness, transparency, privacy and data protection, reliability and security, accountability); (3) toolkits and guardrails for all teams across ideation, build, deployment, and management; (4) 27,600+ employees trained through AI learning series; (5) measurable outcomes — 20M+ payments processed daily with AI fraud detection, 40,355 daily proactive fraud alerts, 20%+ reduction in customer fraud losses H1 FY26 vs H1 FY25; (6) $0.9B FY25 investment in fraud, scam, and cyber protection; (7) partnership with Melbourne Business School for longitudinal AI trust research. The governance forum structure operationalises the 'dedicated governance function' requirement that APRA CPS 230 implies for material model risk. CBA's model demonstrates that formal enterprise-wide AI governance produces measurable outcomes — and that public transparency about governance approach is itself a competitive trust signal.
themes.implications: CBA's model should be the reference architecture for Australian financial services AI governance engagements. The six-principle structure, dedicated governance forum, measurable outcomes, and employee training programme provide the template for any financial institution building enterprise AI governance capability.

themes.name: Mastercard Agent Pay — Production Agentic AI Governance in Australian Financial Services
themes.description: Mastercard's January 2026 Agent Pay launch with CBA and Westpac — Australia's first authenticated agentic transactions — is the leading indicator of what enterprise agentic AI governance looks like in production. Agent Pay provides the authentication and authorisation infrastructure that enables AI agents to execute financial transactions with explicit customer consent and identity verification at agent-customer relationship initiation. Three implications for AI trust research: (1) agentic AI governance is not theoretical — it is in production at Australia's largest financial institutions; (2) trust in agentic transactions is being operationalised through identity and authorisation infrastructure (not algorithmic transparency) — this operationalises Gazit's institutional authority argument; (3) multi-bank participation (CBA + Westpac) demonstrates that AI agent transaction standards require ecosystem-level governance, not just individual firm-level controls. Agent Pay is the reference architecture for financial services agentic AI transaction governance.
themes.implications: Any financial services firm deploying AI agents should model their transaction authentication and authorisation architecture on Agent Pay: explicit consent at relationship initiation, identity verification, transaction-level authorisation with defined scope, and reversal capability. Firms not engaging with agentic AI transaction infrastructure risk being excluded from emerging AI-powered payment ecosystems.

themes.name: Agentic AI Temporal Degradation — Why Certification Intervals Are Structurally Too Long
themes.description: The convergence of four findings from this research session establishes a structural argument: current AI certification intervals are too long for agentic AI systems. (1) CSA (2025) establishes that agentic AI has non-stable identity — agents adapt and change behaviour, meaning a certification anchored to a specific agent state may become invalid as the agent evolves. (2) CSA also establishes that the attack surface of agentic AI changes continuously as agents interact with new environments and tools. (3) CSA identifies cognitive degradation (QSAF framework) as a distinct threat category — agentic AI cognitive capability degrades over time as world models become outdated. (4) CABCA treats change as an expected condition requiring continuous reassessment. The combined implication: for agentic AI, the appropriate certification model is not 'certify at deployment, reassess annually' but 'continuous monitoring with automated trigger-based reassessment.' The annual reassessment model was designed for static software; it is architecturally mismatched to dynamic agentic systems.
themes.implications: Regulators and standards bodies should revise certification interval guidance for agentic AI systems: behavioural properties require continuous monitoring; structural properties can be certified on longer intervals but must be reassessed when the agent's capability level changes substantially.

themes.name: Three-Pillar Liability Framework — the Emerging Standard for AI Accountability
themes.description: The Harvard JOLT three-pillar liability framework (p177) represents the emerging consensus on AI accountability: (1) deployer HSI duty — the entity deploying the AI has a duty to maintain human supervisory involvement proportionate to the system's risk level; (2) developer standards compliance duty — the developer must demonstrate compliance with applicable technical standards at the time of deployment; (3) shared post-market monitoring duty — both deployer and developer share responsibility for post-market monitoring, with allocation determined by who has access to operational data. Combined with trajectory-based liability (p192), the emerging AI accountability architecture has two dimensions: the pillar structure (who owes what duty) and the trajectory structure (what the duty covers). For financial services, this means: deployers need to demonstrate both the structural adequacy of their oversight arrangements (three-pillar) and the behavioural propriety of their AI's actual trajectories (trajectory-based).
themes.implications: Financial services legal and compliance teams should audit their AI systems against both the three-pillar framework and trajectory-based liability requirements. The deployer's HSI duty requires documented evidence of proportionate human supervisory involvement — Reversal Registers (t075) and CABCA-compliant monitoring evidence (t081) are the operational artifacts that satisfy this requirement.

themes.name: Trust Calibration as the Operational Replacement for 'Trustworthy AI'
themes.description: Lugato and Floridi (p166) and McGrath (p196) together establish that 'trustworthy AI' is the wrong target concept. Lugato and Floridi resolve the trust/reliance debate by operationalising trust as a quantitative property of the reliance relationship: appropriate reliance has three components — appropriateness (the reliance is warranted given actual system capabilities, requiring calibration), efficiency (monitoring investment is low relative to value extracted), and calibration (the organisation's trust level matches actual system performance). McGrath validates the measurement: S-TIAS provides a 3-item instrument for measuring whether trust is calibrated. The practical implication: stop asking 'is this AI trustworthy?' and start asking 'is our reliance on this AI appropriately calibrated to its actual performance, and is our monitoring investment proportionate to the value we extract?' The answer to both questions changes continuously — requiring continuous calibration monitoring.
themes.implications: Replace 'trustworthy AI assessment' with 'AI trust calibration audit': measure whether users' trust in each AI system matches actual system performance across the validation dataset, recent production behaviour, and calibration drift. Report calibration error as the key metric. Intervene when calibration error exceeds acceptable thresholds.

themes.name: The Aveni FinLLM Governance Model — Complete Financial Services AI Governance Framework
themes.description: Aveni's governance framework for FinLLM deployment with Lloyds Banking Group and Nationwide Building Society provides the most operationally complete financial services AI governance model available: Principles → Regulations → Standards → Requirements → Safety Report → Artefacts. This progression resolves the most common governance failure mode — the gap between high-level principles and deployable technical requirements. Key operational features: (1) tiered governance structure (AI Governance and Ethics Board → specialised working groups → operational teams); (2) explicit regulatory engagement (FCA Digital Sandbox, University of Edinburgh research partnership, EuroLLM contribution); (3) documentation outputs: Model Cards (HuggingFace), Data Protection Impact Assessments, Model Risk Management Policies, Privacy Notices; (4) hallucination detection dataset development as a specialised safety measure. The Principles-to-Artefacts progression is directly applicable to any financial services AI governance framework development engagement.
themes.implications: Use the Aveni Principles-to-Artefacts progression as the standard engagement structure for AI governance framework development: each layer must be concretely specified, with explicit traceability from principles to the artefacts that evidence their implementation.

themes.name: Proxy-to-Ground-Truth Assumption Gap — The Structural Failure Mode in AI Trust Certification
themes.description: This session's first-principles analysis establishes a structural argument about AI trust certification: the certification chain for AI trust contains three distinct epistemic layers. Ground truth — the actual performance of the AI system — is unknowable in principle for opaque systems and can only be assessed through behavioural testing. Assumptions — the beliefs about AI performance that organisations hold — are typically based on validation results at deployment and degrade as the system encounters novel data. Proxies — the measurable indicators used to stand in for ground truth — include accuracy on test sets, calibration curves, fairness metrics, and drift statistics. The critical structural failure is that certification documents certify proxies (accuracy on a validation dataset measured at a point in time) while users and operators assume they certify ground truth. This gap — certified proxies vs. assumed ground truth — is the primary structural source of AI trust failures. The appropriate response is to make the proxy-to-ground-truth assumption explicit in every certification document and to implement continuous monitoring that detects when the gap between proxy and ground truth has widened beyond acceptable bounds.
evidence: Gazit et al. constitutive knowledge sources (p003)
Lugato and Floridi appropriate reliance (p166)
Balarabe model drift analysis (p013)
CBA governance framework provenance chain (p012)
themes.implications: Every AI assurance engagement should explicitly map: (1) what proxy was measured; (2) under what conditions; (3) what assumptions connect the proxy to actual performance; (4) what the known degradation rate is; (5) what the monitoring frequency is. Certificates should never state accuracy without this proxy qualification. The primary assurance question is: has the gap between certified proxy and actual ground truth widened beyond acceptable bounds?

themes.name: Appropriate Reliance Has Three Operationally Distinct Components — Not One
themes.description: Lugato and Floridi (p166) and McGrath (p196) together establish that 'trustworthy AI' is the wrong target concept, and that appropriate reliance has three operationally distinct components: (1) Appropriateness — the reliance is warranted given the system's actual capabilities, which requires calibration (matching trust level to system trustworthiness); (2) Efficiency — the monitoring investment required to maintain appropriate reliance is low relative to the value extracted, which requires the system to be genuinely reliable and the oversight architecture to be well-designed; (3) Calibration — the organisation's trust level matches the system's actual trustworthiness across different contexts and time periods. The S-TIAS measurement instrument (p196) provides the validated tool for measuring the calibration component. The critical operational insight is that these three components can diverge: a system can be appropriate (within capability boundaries) but inefficient (monitoring costs approach value extracted); or calibrated at one time point and not another. Continuous assurance must monitor all three components, not just accuracy.
evidence: Lugato and Floridi appropriate reliance (p166)
McGrath S-TIAS (p004, p196)
McGrath trust measurement (p004)
themes.implications: Stop asking 'is this AI trustworthy?' and start asking three distinct questions: (1) Is our reliance on this AI within its validated capability boundaries? (2) Is our monitoring investment proportionate to the value we extract? (3) Do our users' trust levels match actual system performance? Each question requires different measurement instruments and governance responses.

themes.name: Human Oversight Fails at Agentic AI Scale Mathematically, Not Just Organisationally
themes.description: The oversight fatigue cascade (Choubey p014, Langer et al.) provides the quantitative logic for why HITL fails at agentic AI scale. The cascade has four stages: (1) automation complacency — human trust in AI increases with AI reliability, decreasing cognitive effort allocated to oversight; (2) alert desensitisation — the ratio of false alarms to genuine signals exceeds human cognitive filtering capacity; (3) reflexive approval — humans apply pattern-matching heuristics without genuine evaluation; (4) accountability ritual — oversight becomes a compliance artifact signed by a human who did not substantively review. Critically, for agentic AI making thousands of decisions per second, the oversight failure is not a governance failure but a mathematical inevitability given the ratio of AI action frequency to human cognitive bandwidth. The resolution-matched supervision architecture (p014) and DAR framework (Westover, p195) are the correct responses: redesign the oversight architecture so that human intervention capacity is matched to the resolution of consequential decisions, not the volume of all decisions.
evidence: OneReach HITL analysis (p014)
Westover DAR framework (p195)
McGrath S-TIAS oversight analysis (p004)
Harvard JOLT negligence standard (p005, p007)
themes.implications: Any AI governance framework that relies on increasing the frequency or depth of human oversight for agentic AI is building on a structurally unsound foundation. The correct response is: (1) acknowledge that oversight will fail at scale; (2) design forensically auditable systems so failures can be detected retroactively; (3) implement resolution-matched supervision as the architectural alternative to oversight-by-review.

themes.name: Institutional Epistemic Trust — AI Trust Requires Social Institutions, Not Just Technical Properties
themes.description: Gazit et al. (p003) make the crucial distinction that resolves many practical confusion in AI trust: when AI systems operate as epistemic instruments — sources of knowledge rather than mere tools — the question of what grounds our trust becomes philosophically distinct from traditional tool trust. The key insight is that institutional epistemic trust requires more than technical accuracy; it requires socially embedded warrant structures: professional certifications, regulatory approvals, peer validation, and institutional accountability chains. This reframes the assurance target: the assurance question is not 'is this AI model accurate?' but 'is the institution deploying and governing this AI system capable of warranted epistemic trust?' For financial services, this translates directly: an AI model's accuracy is necessary but not sufficient for warranted trust; there must be institutional structures that vouch for the model's epistemic validity in specific use contexts. This has profound implications: model risk management must assess institutional governance capacity, not just model performance metrics.
evidence: Gazit et al. constitutive knowledge sources (p003)
Springer AI & Society epistemic trust (p015)
CBA governance formalisation (p012)
DBS Harvard case study (p010)
themes.implications: Assurance engagements should be scoped around institutional governance capacity, not model performance metrics alone. The assessment question is: does this organisation's governance of this AI system justify the reliance stakeholders are placing in it? This requires evaluating decision-rights allocation, escalation paths, monitoring investment, and institutional accountability structures — not just technical model properties.
